content.text: Running a Proof of Concept (PoC) evaluation for an AI vendor is the highest-stakes experiment your organization will conduct before committing significant budget and engineering effort to a production integration. A poorly designed PoC leads to vendor selection based on marketing collateral rather than empirical data — setting up failure months later when real users encounter the system. A rigorous PoC evaluation, by contrast, generates objective, reproducible evidence that your chosen vendor can meet your specific requirements across accuracy, latency, cost, security, and compliance dimensions. This sub-subtopic teaches you to design, execute, and report PoC evaluations that hold up to scrutiny from technical, business, and compliance stakeholders.
What It Is

A PoC evaluation for AI vendors is a time-boxed, structured experiment in which one or more candidate vendors run your representative workloads under controlled conditions, and the results are measured against pre-defined, weighted criteria. It is distinct from a demo (vendor-selected favorable inputs), a benchmark (standardized but not necessarily representative inputs), and a pilot (production-scale rollout with real users). The PoC sits between these: representative but controlled, empirical but scoped.

The evaluation has three planes that must be addressed simultaneously. The technical plane covers model accuracy, inference latency, throughput, and reliability. The economic plane covers per-token cost, per-successful-request cost, and total cost of ownership projections at production scale. The operational and compliance plane covers data handling, logging practices, contractual SLAs, security certifications, and regulatory alignment. Ignoring any plane produces a vendor selection that optimizes one dimension at the cost of another.

PoC evaluation criteria are the explicit, measurable thresholds and weights assigned to each dimension before the evaluation begins. Defining criteria after seeing results — a common anti-pattern — introduces confirmation bias and makes the evaluation non-reproducible. The criteria document must be signed off by all stakeholder groups (technical, business, legal, compliance, security) before any vendor engagement begins.
Why It Matters

Enterprise AI procurement decisions routinely involve multi-year contracts worth hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars. Unlike traditional software procurement, where a vendor's product behavior is largely deterministic and documented, AI model outputs are probabilistic and sensitive to prompt design, model version, context window, and fine-tuning state. A vendor that performs well on the vendor's own demo may perform poorly on your specific data distribution, your edge cases, or your latency requirements under concurrency.

PoC evaluations matter because model capabilities change rapidly. The industry's evaluation landscape shifts quarterly as new model families are released and existing models are updated. A vendor that ranked second in a PoC six months ago may now be the clear leader — or a previously acceptable vendor may have quietly updated a model in a way that degrades performance on your use case. Without a repeatable PoC methodology, organizations lack the ability to re-evaluate vendors on a regular cadence or to challenge vendor claims with their own data.

From a risk management perspective, a documented PoC evaluation creates an audit trail that satisfies procurement, legal, and compliance review requirements. Regulated industries (healthcare, financial services, defense, critical infrastructure) increasingly require documented evidence of AI system evaluation before deployment. The PoC evaluation package — criteria, test data description, results, and decision rationale — serves as this evidence.
Benefits
A well-executed PoC evaluation delivers benefits across multiple organizational dimensions, from immediate risk reduction to long-term vendor relationship management.
Objective vendor ranking:
Weighted scoring based on empirical results replaces gut-feel or relationship-driven vendor selection, making the decision defensible to leadership and procurement.
Risk reduction before commitment:
Discovering that a vendor cannot meet accuracy thresholds during a 6-week PoC costs far less than discovering it after signing a 2-year contract and completing a 6-month integration.
Contract negotiation leverage:

Quantified PoC results give procurement teams hard data to negotiate SLA terms, pricing structures, and performance guarantees — vendors who know they underperformed competitors are more likely to offer concessions.
Team alignment:

A structured PoC forces ML engineers, business analysts, compliance officers, and security engineers to agree on requirements before the evaluation, surfacing misalignment that would otherwise delay production deployment.
Reproducibility:
An automated evaluation harness with pinned datasets and logged results can be re-run against new model versions or challenger vendors without rebuilding the evaluation infrastructure.
Failure mode discovery:
Adversarial and edge-case test inputs surface failure modes — hallucinations, refusals, context truncation errors — before they reach production users.
Regulatory audit readiness:
The PoC evidence package (test protocol, dataset description, results summary, decision memo) satisfies internal audit, external auditor, and regulator requests for AI system evaluation evidence.
Calibrated internal expectations:
Running the evaluation creates realistic expectations across the business about what AI can and cannot do on your specific data, preventing overselling and disappointment post-launch.
Multi-vendor optionality:
Parallel PoC execution across multiple vendors gives procurement the option to select a primary and a backup vendor, reducing single-vendor dependency risk.
Trade-offs and Challenges

PoC evaluations impose real costs and contain inherent limitations that teams must acknowledge. The primary cost is time: a rigorous 4–8 week PoC with automated harness development, dataset labeling, and multi-vendor coordination delays production deployment. This cost is frequently underestimated because teams budget evaluation time without accounting for the dataset preparation effort, which can be the most labor-intensive phase.

A significant challenge is that PoC conditions rarely replicate production conditions exactly. Production traffic distributions, prompt lengths, concurrent user patterns, and context window utilization all differ from PoC test conditions. A vendor that achieves p95 latency of 800ms under controlled PoC conditions may exhibit p95 latency of 2,400ms under production load patterns. Evaluation harnesses must be designed to stress-test vendors at projected production concurrency levels, not just serial request execution.

Vendor cooperation is another challenge. Some vendors are reluctant to participate in parallel PoCs (they prefer exclusive evaluations), may provide limited API access during the PoC period, or may offer their best-performing model version during evaluation and deprioritize it after contract signing. Evaluation criteria must include contractual provisions about model versioning and performance floor guarantees.
Dataset labeling cost:
Creating ground-truth labels for 500–2,000 representative samples requires subject matter expert time, which is expensive and a bottleneck.
Prompt optimization asymmetry:
Teams with deep prompt engineering experience for one vendor's API may inadvertently disadvantage other vendors by not optimizing prompts equivalently for each.
Moving targets:
Vendors update models during the PoC window. Establishing model version pinning and change notification requirements before the evaluation starts is essential.
Criteria weight disputes:
Different stakeholders weight accuracy, cost, and latency differently. Agreeing on weights before results are known is politically difficult but essential for credibility.
Security and data handling constraints:
Using real production data in a PoC may require DPAs, BAAs, or other agreements that take weeks to execute — delaying the PoC start date.
How It Works

The PoC evaluation process follows a five-phase arc: scope definition, dataset preparation, harness execution, metric aggregation, and stakeholder gate review. Each phase has defined inputs, outputs, and responsible roles. The process is explicitly sequential in phases one and two (scope must precede dataset design, which must precede harness development) but parallel in phase three (multiple vendors are evaluated simultaneously by the same harness).

Phase one — scope definition — answers three questions: what use case is being evaluated, what constitutes a successful outcome, and what are the minimum acceptable thresholds for each criterion. The use case should be the single most important AI use case for the initial production deployment, not a comprehensive collection of all possible AI tasks. Scope creep kills PoCs by making dataset preparation unmanageable and evaluation results ambiguous.

Phase two — dataset preparation — is typically the most time-consuming phase. It involves sampling from production data (with appropriate anonymization), augmenting with synthetic edge cases, creating adversarial inputs designed to probe failure modes, and labeling each item with a ground-truth expected output. The labeled dataset is split: a development partition that vendors may use for prompt optimization (optionally), and a held-out test partition that is used only for final scoring.

Phase three — harness execution — runs all vendors concurrently against the test partition using an automated evaluation pipeline. The pipeline sends identical inputs to each vendor API, records response content and timing, computes metrics against ground truth, and stores all results in a structured format for audit. Phase four aggregates metrics into weighted scores per vendor. Phase five presents results to each stakeholder gate (technical, business, compliance, security) for sign-off before vendor selection.
Architecture and Design

The evaluation pipeline architecture separates concerns across four layers: data, execution, measurement, and reporting. This separation ensures that changes to one layer (for example, adding a new vendor adapter) do not affect the measurement or reporting layers, making the harness extensible for future evaluations.

attrs.alt: PoC Evaluation Pipeline showing 5 phases (Define, Prepare, Execute, Measure, Decide), key metrics tracked, and final evaluation report leading to vendor selection

content.text: The data layer holds the annotated test dataset in a versioned, immutable format (e.g., JSONL files with a dataset version hash). The execution layer contains one adapter per vendor that handles authentication, request formatting, retry logic, and response parsing. Each adapter exposes a common interface: send_request(input) -> (response, latency_ms, token_count, cost_usd). The measurement layer consumes adapter outputs and computes all metrics against ground truth. The reporting layer aggregates metrics into per-vendor scorecards and generates the comparative analysis document.
When to Use It

A formal PoC evaluation with structured criteria is warranted whenever the expected contract value exceeds the cost of the evaluation, which for enterprise AI deployments means almost always. However, the depth and formality of the evaluation should scale with the stakes. A low-stakes internal productivity tool evaluation can use a simplified 2-week PoC with informal criteria. A patient-facing healthcare AI system or a credit-decisioning system requires the full 6–8 week evaluation with all four stakeholder gates.
Run a full PoC when:

expected production traffic exceeds 100K requests/day, the use case touches regulated data (PII, PHI, PCI, MNPI), the contract value exceeds $100K annually, or multiple vendors are under consideration.
Run a lightweight PoC when:
the use case is internal-only, the vendor is already approved and this is a capability expansion, traffic is low, and existing usage gives you natural behavioral data to analyze.
Skip the formal PoC when:

the use case is a proof-of-concept exploration (building a prototype before even deciding on a use case), the team needs to fail fast to learn about feasibility, or the vendor selection is constrained by pre-existing enterprise agreements.
Always run when switching vendors:
Switching from an incumbent vendor to a new one requires a PoC that directly compares the challenger against the incumbent's production performance baseline, not against abstract thresholds.
Re-run annually:
The AI vendor landscape changes fast enough that annual re-evaluation, or re-evaluation triggered by a significant model update from either your current vendor or a challenger, is good practice.
How to Implement

Implementation proceeds in six ordered steps. First, convene a PoC kick-off meeting with all four stakeholder groups (ML, business, compliance, security) to ratify the use case scope, the criteria weights, and the pass/fail thresholds. Document the outcome in a signed PoC Charter. Second, negotiate evaluation agreements with each vendor: data processing agreement (DPA), non-disclosure agreement (NDA), API access for the duration, and commitment to model version pinning. Third, prepare the dataset — the most effort-intensive step.

Dataset preparation requires sampling from production data distributions, applying necessary anonymization (de-identification for PHI, masking for PCI), creating synthetic edge cases to test specific failure modes, creating adversarial inputs designed to probe bias or hallucination, and labeling every item with its ground-truth expected output reviewed by a subject matter expert. A minimum viable PoC dataset for a text classification or generation use case contains 500 items; 1,500–2,000 items gives statistical confidence on subgroup performance.

Fourth, build the evaluation harness. The harness must be run against all vendors identically — same test items in the same order, same timeout thresholds, same retry policy, same concurrency level. Fifth, execute the evaluation during a pre-agreed window, collecting results for all vendors simultaneously. Sixth, compute weighted scores, produce per-vendor scorecards, and present findings at each stakeholder gate before making a final selection recommendation.

Below is a representative Python evaluation harness (~60 lines) that demonstrates async parallel evaluation with metric collection and per-vendor report generation. This harness uses a common vendor adapter interface pattern and produces a structured JSON result object.

import asyncio
import time
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class EvalResult:
    vendor: str
    item_id: str
    response: str
    latency_ms: float
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cost_usd: float
    passed: bool = False
    error: str = ""

@dataclass
class VendorReport:
    vendor: str
    total_items: int
    pass_count: int
    precision: float
    recall: float
    f1: float
    p50_latency_ms: float
    p95_latency_ms: float
    p99_latency_ms: float
    total_cost_usd: float
    cost_per_successful_req: float
    error_rate: float
    weighted_score: float = 0.0

async def evaluate_vendor(
    vendor_name: str,
    adapter,
    dataset: list[dict],
    ground_truth: dict[str, str],
    semaphore: asyncio.Semaphore,
) -> list[EvalResult]:
    results = []
    async with semaphore:
        tasks = [call_vendor(vendor_name, adapter, item, ground_truth) for item in dataset]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    return [r for r in results if isinstance(r, EvalResult)]

async def call_vendor(vendor_name, adapter, item, ground_truth) -> EvalResult:
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        resp = await adapter.send(item["input"])
        latency = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
        passed = evaluate_output(resp.text, ground_truth[item["id"]])
        return EvalResult(
            vendor=vendor_name, item_id=item["id"],
            response=resp.text, latency_ms=latency,
            input_tokens=resp.input_tokens, output_tokens=resp.output_tokens,
            cost_usd=resp.cost_usd, passed=passed
        )
    except Exception as exc:
        return EvalResult(
            vendor=vendor_name, item_id=item["id"],
            response="", latency_ms=-1, input_tokens=0, output_tokens=0,
            cost_usd=0, error=str(exc)
        )

def compute_report(results: list[EvalResult], weights: dict) -> VendorReport:
    import statistics
    passed = [r for r in results if r.passed]
    latencies = sorted([r.latency_ms for r in results if r.latency_ms > 0])
    n = len(latencies)
    p50 = latencies[int(n * 0.50)] if latencies else 0
    p95 = latencies[int(n * 0.95)] if latencies else 0
    p99 = latencies[int(n * 0.99)] if latencies else 0
    total_cost = sum(r.cost_usd for r in results)
    error_count = sum(1 for r in results if r.error)
    precision = len(passed) / len(results) if results else 0
    report = VendorReport(
        vendor=results[0].vendor if results else "",
        total_items=len(results), pass_count=len(passed),
        precision=precision, recall=precision, f1=precision,
        p50_latency_ms=p50, p95_latency_ms=p95, p99_latency_ms=p99,
        total_cost_usd=total_cost,
        cost_per_successful_req=total_cost / len(passed) if passed else float('inf'),
        error_rate=error_count / len(results) if results else 1.0
    )
    report.weighted_score = (
        weights["accuracy"] * precision +
        weights["latency"] * max(0, 1 - p95 / 2000) +
        weights["cost"] * max(0, 1 - total_cost / 100)
    )
    return report

async def run_poc(vendors: dict, dataset: list, ground_truth: dict, weights: dict):
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10)
    vendor_tasks = {
        name: evaluate_vendor(name, adapter, dataset, ground_truth, sem)
        for name, adapter in vendors.items()
    }
    all_results = await asyncio.gather(*vendor_tasks.values())
    reports = [
        compute_report(results, weights)
        for results in all_results
    ]
    reports.sort(key=lambda r: r.weighted_score, reverse=True)
    print(json.dumps([asdict(r) for r in reports], indent=2))
    return reports

The YAML configuration below shows how evaluation criteria, weights, and pass/fail thresholds are expressed declaratively, keeping them separate from the evaluation code and making them auditable by non-engineers.

# poc-config.yaml — PoC evaluation criteria and thresholds
poc:
  name: "Clinical Documentation Assistant — LLM Vendor Evaluation"
  version: "2024-Q3-v1"
  dataset:
    path: "data/clinical_notes_test_v3.jsonl"
    version_hash: "sha256:a3f9..."
    size: 1500
    split: { dev: 300, test: 1200 }
  vendors:
    - id: vendor_a
      model: "gpt-4o-2024-08-06"
      max_concurrency: 10
    - id: vendor_b
      model: "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
      max_concurrency: 10
    - id: vendor_c
      model: "gemini-1.5-pro-002"
      max_concurrency: 8
  criteria:
    accuracy:
      weight: 0.40
      threshold_pass: 0.88     # F1 >= 0.88 required to pass
      threshold_target: 0.93
      metric: f1_score
    latency_p95:
      weight: 0.20
      threshold_pass: 2000     # p95 <= 2000ms required
      threshold_target: 800
      unit: ms
      lower_is_better: true
    cost_per_1k_tokens:
      weight: 0.15
      threshold_pass: 0.030    # <= $0.030/1K tokens required
      threshold_target: 0.015
      unit: usd
      lower_is_better: true
    throughput_rps:
      weight: 0.10
      threshold_pass: 50       # >= 50 req/sec at peak
      threshold_target: 200
      unit: requests_per_second
    error_rate:
      weight: 0.10
      threshold_pass: 0.005    # <= 0.5% error rate
      threshold_target: 0.001
      lower_is_better: true
    compliance_score:
      weight: 0.05
      threshold_pass: 1.0      # All compliance checks must pass (binary)
      metric: manual_checklist
  gates:
    technical:
      required_criteria: [accuracy, latency_p95, throughput_rps, error_rate]
      approvers: [ml_lead, platform_lead]
    business:
      required_criteria: [cost_per_1k_tokens, accuracy]
      approvers: [product_owner, finance_lead]
    compliance:
      required_criteria: [compliance_score]
      approvers: [compliance_officer, privacy_counsel]
    security:
      required_criteria: [compliance_score]
      approvers: [ciso, security_engineer]
  timeline:
    weeks: 6
    checkpoints:
      - week: 2
        milestone: "Dataset finalized and labeled; harness built"
      - week: 4
        milestone: "Preliminary results; stakeholder checkpoint #1"
      - week: 6
        milestone: "Final results; all gate reviews; vendor decision"
  disqualifying_conditions:
    - any_criterion_below_threshold: true
    - vendor_model_change_without_notice: true
    - data_handling_violation: true
Examples
The following examples illustrate how PoC evaluation criteria are applied in different enterprise contexts with different priority weightings.
Example 1 — Customer support triage classification:

A telecommunications company wants to classify incoming support tickets into 48 intent categories for routing to the correct team. The PoC criteria weight accuracy at 50% (misrouted tickets increase handle time), latency at 25% (the classification must complete within 500ms to fit in the ticket ingestion pipeline), cost at 15%, and reliability at 10%. Vendors are evaluated on 2,000 tickets with SME-labeled categories. The minimum passing F1 is 0.92 for top-3 accuracy (the ticket is classified into the top-3 categories and the correct one is present).
Example 2 — Contract clause extraction for legal review:

A private equity firm evaluates LLMs for extracting 12 specific clause types (indemnification, limitation of liability, change of control, etc.) from M&A target company contracts. Precision is weighted more heavily than recall (15% false positive rate is acceptable; 5% false negative rate is not). The PoC dataset contains 300 contracts with 4,200 labeled clause instances. Latency requirements are relaxed (contracts are reviewed overnight, not in real time), so cost and accuracy dominate the criteria weights.
Example 3 — Multi-vendor A/B comparison for RAG summarization:

An investment bank evaluates three LLMs as the synthesis layer in a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system for equity research. Evaluation criteria include factual grounding rate (percentage of claims in the generated summary that can be attributed to retrieved passages), citation accuracy, completeness (fraction of key facts from retrieved passages included in summary), and latency. Because the output is free text rather than a classification, automatic metrics are supplemented with a human evaluation panel of two senior analysts who score 100 randomly sampled outputs per vendor on a 1–5 rubric.
Real-World Example

A mid-sized regional health system with 12 hospitals decided to deploy an AI-assisted clinical documentation system that would generate draft SOAP notes from physician-patient conversation audio transcripts. The system would be used by 800 physicians across internal medicine, emergency, and surgical specialties. Given the PHI involved and the clinical risk of inaccurate notes, the evaluation team convened a cross-functional working group: two ML engineers, one NLP research scientist, one clinical informatics officer (a practicing physician), one privacy and compliance officer, one cybersecurity engineer, and one finance analyst.

Five vendors were shortlisted from an initial RFI process: three general-purpose LLM API providers (Vendor A: GPT-4o, Vendor B: Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Vendor C: Gemini 1.5 Pro) and two healthcare-specialized LLM platforms (Vendor D: a clinically fine-tuned model on de-identified EHR data, Vendor E: a smaller fine-tuned model with a SOC 2 Type II + HIPAA BAA).

The evaluation ran for 7 weeks. Week 1 was devoted to executing BAAs with all five vendors and preparing the test dataset: 1,800 de-identified transcript-note pairs labeled by two physician reviewers, with inter-rater agreement measured by Cohen's kappa (target kappa > 0.80). Week 2 built the evaluation harness and validated it against a development partition. Weeks 3–5 executed the evaluation, with the team meeting weekly to review preliminary results and address any harness bugs. Week 6 ran the four stakeholder gates. Week 7 produced the final vendor selection memo with contract recommendations.

Results: Vendor B achieved the highest clinical accuracy score (SOAP section completeness: 91%, factual error rate: 1.2%), but exceeded the latency threshold at p95 (3,100ms vs. the 2,000ms pass threshold). Vendor D, the clinically fine-tuned model, achieved 89% completeness and 0.9% factual error rate with p95 latency of 1,400ms and passed all gates. Vendor E failed the accuracy gate but passed compliance and security. Vendors A and C failed either accuracy or compliance gates. The health system selected Vendor D as primary and Vendor B as a strategic backup, contingent on Vendor B's meeting the latency SLA with dedicated throughput tier pricing. The PoC evidence package (protocol, dataset description, anonymized results, decision memo) was filed with the health system's AI governance committee.
Common Mistakes

The most damaging mistakes in PoC evaluations are those that produce misleading results — causing the organization to select a vendor that will fail in production, or disqualify a vendor that would have succeeded.
Defining criteria after seeing results:

The most common bias-introducing mistake. If the team sees that Vendor A scores 91% accuracy and Vendor B scores 88%, and then sets the accuracy threshold at 90%, the evaluation has been reverse-engineered to justify a predetermined preference. Criteria must be signed off before any evaluation run.
Testing only happy-path inputs:

A dataset composed entirely of clean, well-formatted, typical inputs will give misleadingly high accuracy scores. Production traffic always contains malformed inputs, ambiguous requests, very long contexts, and adversarial edge cases.
Serial rather than concurrent evaluation:
Running evaluation requests serially (one at a time) dramatically underestimates latency under production concurrency. Use the expected production concurrency level during harness execution.
Unequal prompt optimization effort:

Spending two weeks optimizing prompts for Vendor A and using a default prompt for Vendor B creates an unfair comparison. Each vendor should receive equivalent prompt engineering effort, or all vendors should use a standard prompt.
Not pinning model versions:
Vendor model updates mid-PoC invalidate earlier results. Require vendors to commit to a specific model version identifier for the duration and to notify you immediately of any changes.
Ignoring token cost asymmetry:

Vendors differ significantly in how they count tokens (some count system prompts in every request, others cache them). Total cost comparisons must account for the actual billing model for your expected usage pattern.
Excluding compliance from the PoC:

Technical teams often evaluate accuracy and latency without looping in compliance or security until after a vendor is selected. Discovering BAA or DPA issues after selection wastes evaluation effort and delays procurement.
Treating all errors equally:

A refusal (the model declines to answer) and a hallucination (the model confidently gives a wrong answer) are not equally harmful for most use cases. Precision and recall should be broken down by error type.
No baseline comparison:

Every PoC should include a non-AI baseline (e.g., current rule-based system, human performance, or a simple keyword classifier). Without a baseline, you cannot determine whether the evaluated LLMs provide sufficient improvement to justify the cost.
Best Practices

These practices, collected from enterprise AI procurement teams across multiple industries, consistently differentiate evaluations that produce actionable, defensible results from those that end in disagreement or poor vendor selection.
Write the vendor selection memo before the evaluation:

Draft the decision document with all criteria, weights, and thresholds filled in, but leave the results blank. This forces the team to fully specify what a successful outcome looks like before seeing any data.
Use a stratified dataset:

Partition the test dataset into at least three strata: typical cases (60%), difficult cases (25%), and adversarial/edge cases (15%). Report metrics separately by stratum so you can see how vendors differ on hard inputs, not just average performance.
Measure inter-rater agreement on labeled data:

Always compute Cohen's kappa or Fleiss' kappa on your ground-truth labels before running the evaluation. Labels with kappa < 0.70 indicate ambiguous criteria that will make accuracy results uninterpretable.
Report confidence intervals, not point estimates:
With 1,200 test items, a 95% confidence interval on F1 of ±0.025 means that two vendors scoring 0.91 and 0.89 are not statistically distinguishable. Present CI bounds to avoid false precision.
Stress-test at projected production concurrency:
Run a load test phase at the expected peak concurrency (e.g., 50 concurrent requests). Latency figures from a serial or low-concurrency evaluation will be optimistic.
Include a failure mode analysis report:

For each vendor that fails a criterion, document specifically what failed and why. This supports the vendor negotiation (can the vendor offer a remediation?) and informs the risk discussion for any vendor selected despite marginal criterion performance.
Time-box ruthlessly:

A PoC that runs past its planned end date loses credibility and creates organizational fatigue. Six weeks is the maximum for a full enterprise PoC. If the team cannot complete dataset preparation in time, reduce dataset size rather than extend the timeline.
Archive all artifacts:

Store the evaluation harness code, dataset version, all raw API responses, computed metrics, and the final report in a versioned artifact store. This enables re-running the evaluation against new model versions and responding to audit inquiries.
Negotiate model performance floors into contracts:

Use PoC results as the contractual performance floor. For example: 'Vendor warrants that production performance on the agreed benchmark suite will not fall below F1=0.87 on the evaluation test partition for the duration of the contract.'
Performance and Scalability

Performance evaluation in a PoC has two distinct dimensions: measuring vendor performance under controlled conditions, and designing your evaluation harness to run efficiently at scale. On the vendor measurement side, the key metrics are accuracy (F1, precision, recall broken down by class and stratum), latency (p50, p95, p99 in milliseconds — not averages, which mask tail behavior), throughput (sustained requests per second at production concurrency), and cost.

Latency percentiles deserve special attention. P50 (median) latency is what half of users experience. P95 latency is what the 95th-percentile user experiences — typically 2–5x higher than p50 for LLM APIs. P99 latency can be 10–20x higher than p50 due to request queuing, rate limiting, and model cold-start phenomena. Most production SLA commitments should be defined against p95, not p50, and your evaluation must measure p99 to understand worst-case user experience.

Throughput measurement requires running requests at your expected production concurrency level (e.g., 50 concurrent users), not serially. Vendors impose rate limits (requests per minute, tokens per minute) that may be sufficient for your development usage but insufficient for production peak load. The PoC must explicitly test whether the vendor's available tier for your expected spend level can sustain the required throughput.

Cost measurement must reflect your actual production usage pattern. If your production use case involves long system prompts that are reused across requests, measure cost with prompt caching enabled (where available). Compare cost-per-successful-request rather than cost-per-token in isolation — a vendor with higher per-token costs but higher accuracy may be cheaper at the request level because it requires fewer retries and produces fewer downstream errors that require human remediation.
Tooling and Ecosystem

The evaluation tooling ecosystem has matured significantly since 2023, offering options ranging from fully custom harnesses to managed evaluation platforms. The right tooling choice depends on your team's engineering capacity, the sensitivity of your evaluation data, and the required depth of customization.
LangSmith (LangChain):

Provides evaluation dataset management, automated metric computation, and comparison dashboards. Well-integrated with LangChain/LangGraph applications. Supports custom evaluator functions and human-in-the-loop annotation.
Weights & Biases (W&B) Weave:
Designed for ML experiment tracking but increasingly used for LLM evaluation. Tracks all inputs, outputs, and metrics with full lineage. Good for teams already using W&B for model training.
Braintrust:

Purpose-built for LLM evaluation with multi-vendor support, dataset versioning, and human annotation workflows. Offers both SaaS and self-hosted deployment options, which is important for PHI-sensitive evaluations.
Promptfoo:

Open-source CLI tool for testing LLM prompts across providers. YAML-configured, runs locally, and supports red-teaming (adversarial input generation). Well-suited for teams that need air-gapped evaluation environments.
Eleuther LM Evaluation Harness:
The reference open-source harness for academic LLM benchmarking. Covers 200+ standard benchmarks. Useful as a supplement when you want to compare your PoC results against published model performance.
RAGAS:
Specialized for evaluating RAG systems. Measures faithfulness, answer relevancy, context precision, and context recall using an LLM-as-judge approach. Essential for RAG-based PoC evaluations.
Custom Python harnesses:

For highly regulated industries (healthcare, defense) where data cannot leave on-premises or where vendor-specific API behaviors require custom adapters, a custom async harness (like the example above) built on httpx and asyncio gives full control with no data sovereignty concerns.
Label management:

Scale AI, Labelbox, and Prodigy are commonly used for ground-truth labeling with SME reviewers. These platforms support annotation quality controls, inter-annotator agreement measurement, and label versioning.
Result storage and audit trail:

Store raw API responses in append-only object storage (S3, GCS) with versioned dataset references. DuckDB is useful for local analytical queries on evaluation result JSONL files without requiring a database server.
Comparison with Alternatives

Organizations evaluating AI vendors can choose among three primary approaches: manual expert evaluation, a structured PoC with an automated harness (the approach described in this topic), and reliance on third-party benchmarks. Each approach has a distinct cost-benefit profile.
Dimension
Manual Expert Evaluation

Enterprise vendor selection
Initial shortlisting

The recommended pattern for enterprise vendor selection combines all three approaches sequentially: use third-party benchmarks (MMLU, HELM, MT-Bench, domain-specific leaderboards) to shortlist 3–5 vendors from the full market; run the automated PoC harness on your data to rank shortlisted vendors on technical criteria; and use a targeted manual expert review of 50–100 PoC outputs to evaluate qualitative dimensions (tone, format compliance, handling of ambiguity) that automated metrics may miss.
Security and Reliability

Security and reliability evaluation is a distinct gate in the PoC process, separate from technical accuracy and performance measurement. This gate is owned by the security engineer and compliance officer, and it covers three domains: data handling security, model reliability and availability, and contractual risk.

Data handling security assessment examines whether the vendor's API offers data-in-transit encryption (TLS 1.2+ required, 1.3 preferred), whether request and response data is logged by the vendor and for how long, whether the vendor uses customer data to train future models (a disqualifier for most enterprise use cases with proprietary data), what geographic regions data is processed in (EU data residency requirements, for example), and what third-party security certifications the vendor holds (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, HIPAA BAA availability).

Reliability measurement during the PoC should include tracking the vendor's actual uptime over the evaluation window (cross-reference against the vendor's public status page), the error rate (HTTP 5xx responses, timeout rate, rate-limit error rate), and behavior under rate-limit conditions (does the API return a structured error with retry-after headers, or does it silently degrade?). Review the vendor's historical incident log for the past 12 months, focusing on duration and severity of outages.
Required security certifications to verify:
SOC 2 Type II (not just Type I — Type II covers operational effectiveness), ISO 27001, and HIPAA BAA (for healthcare). Request the actual certificates, not just vendor attestations.
Data residency requirements:

Confirm processing geography. Many enterprise agreements require that EU customer data be processed in EU regions only, and vendors differ in whether they offer in-region processing for all tiers or only enterprise tiers.
Prompt injection testing:

Include a prompt injection test suite in your adversarial evaluation partition. Test whether the model can be manipulated into ignoring system prompt instructions via user input crafted to override system-level directives.
SLA contractual coverage:

Evaluate whether the vendor's commercial SLA covers the uptime and latency thresholds that pass your technical gate. A vendor with 99.9% uptime SLA vs. one with 99.5% makes a material difference at production scale.
Model versioning and deprecation policy:

Understand the vendor's policy on model deprecation (how much notice? How long is the deprecated model available in parallel?). Sudden model deprecation without adequate migration time is a reliability risk.
Mental Model

The most useful mental model for PoC evaluation is the clinical trial design framework adapted for software. A clinical trial has a pre-registered protocol (your PoC Charter with predefined criteria), a controlled intervention (vendor A API vs. vendor B API), a primary endpoint (F1 score on the test partition), secondary endpoints (latency, cost, reliability), and a data safety monitoring board that reviews interim results and can halt the trial if a vendor clearly dominates or fails — analogous to your weekly checkpoint reviews.

This framing makes clear why the sequence matters: pre-registration before data collection prevents p-hacking (trying different metrics until one favors your preferred vendor). Blinding is also useful where possible: have the metric computation run against anonymized vendor IDs during the evaluation window, with de-anonymization occurring only at the stakeholder gate review. This prevents cognitive anchoring where a reviewer's pre-existing vendor preference influences their interpretation of ambiguous results.

A second useful mental model is the three-gate factory inspection: your PoC is a quality assurance process for a product you are about to install in your factory (your production system). The technical gate inspects whether the product meets the engineering specification. The business gate inspects whether the product is economically viable. The compliance and security gate inspects whether the product is safe to operate in your environment. All three gates must pass before the product ships — not two of three, not an average of all three.

The weighted scoring model is used for ranking vendors that pass all gates — it determines which acceptable vendor is best, not whether a vendor is acceptable. Conflating the threshold check (pass/fail per gate) with the weighted score (ranking among passing vendors) is a common conceptual mistake that allows a vendor to 'score well overall' despite failing a critical threshold. Always apply threshold checks before weighted ranking.
Key Takeaways

Define all criteria, weights, and pass/fail thresholds in a signed PoC Charter before engaging any vendor and before viewing any evaluation results — post-hoc criterion adjustment is the most common source of biased vendor selection.

A PoC dataset should be stratified into typical, difficult, and adversarial partitions, with a minimum of 500 labeled items and ground-truth labels with inter-rater agreement (kappa > 0.70) validated before the evaluation begins.

Measure latency as p95 and p99, not averages — LLM API latency distributions have long right tails, and average latency is misleading about production user experience for the 5–10% of requests that are slowest.

Run the evaluation harness at your expected production concurrency level, not serially — latency and throughput characteristics change significantly under concurrent load due to API rate limiting and queuing behavior.

The four stakeholder gates (technical, business, compliance, security) are threshold checks — a vendor must pass all four to proceed to weighted ranking. They are not components of a weighted average.

Use YAML-based evaluation configuration files to keep criteria, weights, thresholds, and dataset references separate from harness code — this allows non-engineers to review and sign off on evaluation parameters without reading Python.

An automated PoC harness that archives all raw API responses, computed metrics, and dataset version hashes creates an audit trail that satisfies regulatory review and can be re-run against new model versions to track vendor performance over time.

Compare cost-per-successful-request, not cost-per-token in isolation — a vendor that costs more per token but achieves higher accuracy may be cheaper overall because it avoids downstream costs of errors and human remediation.

Third-party benchmarks (MMLU, HELM, domain-specific leaderboards) are useful for initial vendor shortlisting but should not substitute for PoC evaluation on your specific data — public benchmarks rarely represent your domain, data distribution, or operational constraints.

PoC results should directly inform contract terms: F1 and latency floor guarantees in the SLA, model version change notification requirements, data handling restrictions, and deprecation notice windows should all be derived from what the PoC measured and agreed as minimums.
